title: Slopes in eigenvarieties for definite unitary groups
speaker: Lynnelle Ye
abstract: The study of eigenvarieties began with Coleman and Mazur, who constructed the first eigencurve, a rigid analytic space parametrizing $p$-adic modular Hecke eigenforms. Since then various authors have constructed eigenvarieties for automorphic forms on many other groups. We will give bounds on the eigenvalues of the $U_p$ Hecke operator appearing in Chenevier's eigenvarieties for definite unitary groups.

proof_stages.label: Definite unitary automorphic-form setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates eigenvarieties, fixes a definite unitary group compact at infinity and split at p, and defines the relevant p-adic spaces of automorphic forms with weight and level.

proof_stages.label: Compact U_p operator and Chenevier eigenvariety
proof_stages.summary: The completely continuous Hecke operator U_p is introduced, weight space is described as a union of open polydisks, and the eigenvariety is identified with pairs of weights and nonzero U_p-eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Higher-rank boundary-slope theorem
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the Coleman–Mazur boundary-slope conjecture and rank-two results, the speaker encodes higher-rank slopes in Newton polygons and states polynomial upper and lower bounds near the boundary, with a partial geometric consequence.

proof_stages.label: Lower bound via Fredholm coefficients
proof_stages.summary: The lower Newton-polygon bound is reduced to valuation estimates for coefficients of the Fredholm determinant det(1-xU_p), which in turn follow from explicit matrix-coefficient estimates made uniform near the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Upper bound reduced to many small-slope classical forms
proof_stages.summary: To force the Newton polygon below a point, the proof seeks many linearly independent eigenforms of bounded slope, using locally algebraic classical subspaces and companion-form symmetry to bound finite slopes.

proof_stages.label: Principal-series detection of nonzero U_p
proof_stages.summary: The condition U_pf≠0 is reinterpreted locally as the p-component of the associated automorphic representation being principal series, and Roche’s principal-series types give a criterion in terms of a J–chi vector.

proof_stages.label: Construction and counting of the principal-series subspace
proof_stages.summary: By proving an induced finite-group representation is irreducible and inserting it into the automorphic-form definition, the proof constructs a large subspace forcing J–chi vectors, counts it combinatorially to obtain the upper bound, and identifies it with the minimal-level principal-series part.

examples.name: Strong-approximation contrast
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the compact-at-infinity unitary groups with groups satisfying strong approximation, where density of rational points would make the displayed continuous-function model impossible.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses a hypothetical strong-approximation case only as a contrast, stating that density would prevent nontrivial continuous functions

passes.reason: an ordinary strong-approximation case is invoked as a density obstruction to the displayed automorphic-form construction
Strong approximation makes the rational points dense, collapsing the continuous quotient/function theory and serving as an obstruction to the displayed automorphic-form definition.
a strong-approximation group is invoked as an obstruction where G(Q) would be dense in G(A_f) and the proposed definition would fail

examples.name: GL2 local representation trichotomy
examples.note: The principal/special/supercuspidal classification for smooth representations of GL2(Qp), together with Casselman new vectors, illustrates why a brute-force local argument works in rank two but not in higher rank.
examples.engagement_reason: the GL2 trichotomy is enumerated and cited as allowing a brute-force check, but the check is not actually performed

passes.reason: the GL2 trichotomy is a minimal-rank classification used to recognize principal series by brute force
The GL2 local representation trichotomy is the minimal low-rank classification with only principal-series, special, and supercuspidal cases, unlike the higher-rank Bernstein proliferation.
the GL2 local representation trichotomy is the minimal-rank classification with only three types, used to enable a brute-force argument
the GL2 trichotomy is the low-rank boundary classification with only the three standard types, used as a tractable comparison
the GL2 trichotomy is the minimal nonabelian local GL_n classification with only three representation types, used as a simplifying base case
